content:
The smallest unit in AI is not a word. Once you get the token, half of AI stops feeling like magic and starts feeling like a system with a meter running.

4 min read
Just now

--

New here? Quick Tech Trends is my Thursday series where I take one tech term, old or new, and explain it in plain words. No jargon walls, no filler. This week: what a token actually is.
A token is not a word. That is the first thing to unlearn.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Raj Rana on UnsplashEveryone assumes AI reads words the way we do. It doesn’t. It reads tokens. And almost everything about how these models are priced, limited, and measured runs through this one little unit.
Here is my take on it: if you understand tokens, you understand the economics of AI. If you don’t, AI bills will keep surprising you.
What a token actually is?
A token is a chunk of text. Sometimes a whole word, sometimes part of a word, sometimes just a space or a punctuation mark.
The rough rule of thumb in English: one token is about four characters, or roughly three-quarters of a word.
Let’s count one. Take the sentence “Product management is fun.” Common words like “is” and “fun” are usually one token each. Longer or rarer words get split into pieces. Something like “tokenization” might break into two or three tokens because the model has never seen it often enough to store it whole.
So a 100-word paragraph is not 100 tokens. It is closer to 130 or 140. Spaces count. Punctuation counts. Numbers count.
Why tokens matter so much in AI?
Because the token is the atom of the entire system. Three things all run on it.
You pay per token. Not per question, not per month of usage, per token in and per token out. A model might charge, say, $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens. Send a 500-token prompt, get a 500-token answer, and one call costs a tiny fraction of a cent. Now run that a million times a month and you are into real money. The verbose prompt you copy-pasted “just to be safe” is a line item.
The context window is measured in tokens. How much the model can hold in mind at once is a token count, not a word count. More on that in a separate post.
Speed is measured in tokens. When people say a model runs at 80 tokens per second, that is how fast it types back at you.
And underneath all of it, the model literally works by predicting the next token, then the next, then the next. Tokens are not just how we bill AI. They are how AI thinks.
How the token came to be?
Models can’t read letters or words directly. They only do math on numbers. So text has to be converted into numbers first, and someone had to decide what the unit of conversion should be.
Early systems used whole words. The problem: the vocabulary got enormous, and the model choked on any word it had never seen.
Then people tried single characters. Too granular. The model spent all its effort just reassembling letters into meaning.
The fix was the middle path, subword tokenization (the common method is called Byte Pair Encoding). Frequent chunks stay whole, rare words get chopped into smaller known pieces. That is exactly why “the” is one token and “tokenization” is three. The system keeps what it sees often and breaks down what it doesn’t.
A few things nobody tells you about tokens
Non-English text costs more. Hindi, Chinese, Arabic, and many other languages use more tokens per word than English. So a translated app can quietly cost two or three times as much for the same message. If you are building for global users, this shows up on the bill.
Every model tokenizes differently. The same sentence can be a different number of tokens on two different models. Comparing prices without comparing tokenizers is comparing nothing.
This is why AI is bad at counting letters. Ask a model how many times the letter “r” appears in “strawberry” and it often fumbles. It doesn’t see letters. It sees tokens. You are asking it to count something it never really looked at.
The takeaway
The token is three things at once: the currency you pay in, the ruler everything is measured by, and the atom the model actually reasons in.
Once you see it, the mystery drains out of AI pricing and AI limits. There is a meter running under every conversation, and now you know what it is counting.
Next time your AI bill looks strange, don’t start with the model. Start with the tokens.

If this made tokens click, follow me here on Medium for the rest of the Quick Tech Trends series. I post the shorter takes on LinkedIn and X through the week.
